data.title: Newmont: The Silver Medal In Gold Mining, And That's Not A Bad Thing
data.description:
Newmont Corporation (NEM) is the world's largest gold miner with multi-metal assets including copper, silver, zinc, and lead. It often suffers from comparisons with (relatively) leaner and meaner operations like Agnico and criticised for being inefficient - with increasing costs, riskier multi-jurisdictional assets, and low returns per share over the last couple of decades. While there is merit to this criticism, it overlooks the fact that an existing behemoth's playbook is necessarily different, and often singularly unique, from that of a player from a newer generation, like Agnico. Further, it looks like Newmont is wrapping up a nearly two-decade period of spending and dilution and is ready to start generating value - which is good news for investors who have been waiting a long time to see growth in this stock.

NEM's empire-building years, and their cost

Newmont's main rivals in the gold mining business are Agnico, Barrick, and AngloGold. At 5.3 million ounces, NEM's gold production guidance for 2026 far exceeds its rivals, who are expected to produce roughly between 3-3.5 million ounces. However, it has the least profitable operating margin of the lot at ~29%. By comparison, Agnico, famous for its efficiency, has a margin of ~49%, while the other two are between 25% and 30%.

Meanwhile, the total shareholder return (TSR i.e., growth and re-invested dividend) over 10 years is calculated in the table below:

Asset/Company

10-Year Total Return

CAGR

Agnico Eagle (AEM)

+442%

~18.4%

Spot Gold (USD)

+260%

~13.7%

Newmont

+258%

~13.6%

S&P 500 (Total Return)

+285%

~14.4%

AngloGold Ashanti (AU)

+232%

~12.7%

Barrick Gold (GOLD)

+188%

~11.2%

Most of the usual criticism of NEM is aimed at the lacklustre TSR and low profit margins. As a separate point, we can also add the jurisdictional risk of nearly half of its revenue generation being from non-Tier 1 jurisdictions like Papua New Guinea (PNG), Ghana, and Mexico.

First, the TSR. As you can see, NEM is an underperformer in two contexts - one, against spot gold, and two, against Agnico's stellar run. The second point is undebatable - Agnico has been a darling for a reason and blown the competition out of the water over the past two decades in terms of returns. However, I will explain below why I think we are at an inflection point now where NEM might just be coming out of the woods as a strong independent play.

The first point - that NEM has underperformed the gold spot prices is - I feel - neither here nor there. Investors buy mining stocks and spot gold for two entirely different investment profiles. I have brought it up here only to note that direct TSR comparisons with the spot price of gold have been a source of low-hanging criticism and bad optics for NEM, but such comparisons don't quite make sense. Increasing gold prices will eventually reflect in the company's P/NAV, but we are still in the middle of that process.

Now, to analyse the TSR, we look at NEM's era of empire building. NEM has seen two massive acquisitions and share dilutions over the last decade. The Goldcorp acquisition diluted the total shareholding from 534 million shares to 819 million in Q1 2019. This was slimmed down slightly to 795 million by 2023 when NEM executed the Newcrest acquisition, the largest gold mining takeover in history, further increasing the share count to 1.15 billion shares. Since then, NEM has executed a large buyback (62 million shares) and reduced its total share count to 1.086 billion shares. This is a total dilution of 103% from 2016.

The comparison with its main rivals is interesting. Agnico has diluted its shareholding even more, up to 125% in the last decade, and yet managed to provide massively better 10-year TSR (see table). Even more interestingly, Barrick Gold and AngloGold have diluted their shareholding by less than half of what NEM has and yet delivered lower TSR in the 10-year period (even though they have both seen good price growth over the last year). Barrick has seen massive growth in 2025, and seems to have shown good numbers recently, but its troubles have also been fairly public, and until I see a coherent plan that involves something more than pulling out of Mali and Pakistan, I wouldn't touch it right now. AngloGold is a more interesting case. AU has managed to close out a difficult hedge book, and then opted to exit from South Africa, before starting a new offensive phase with the Centamin Acquisition and the Arthur Project.

So, the picture this creates for us based on past performance is an interesting one - Agnico has dominated without doubt, with its terrific value accretion from the merger-of-equals. Barrick is, in my opinion, still trying to figure out its act. For me, this leaves AU and NEM as two competitors for the silver in gold, but both represent different investment profiles. AU is trying to become a leaner pure-play in gold. NEM, on the other hand, is leaning into its size, diversifying into a polymetallic play even as it maintains the world's largest gold output in sheer volume. AU's growth depends now on a limited number of factors - on managing its new discovery and on the underlying asset price of gold - and its risk-reward profile looks a bit more like that of a junior developer. NEM, on the other hand, is a larger, more diverse, operation whose medium-term growth depends upon improving its overall operating margins rather than a binary outcome for any single, or even a few, assets. The good news is that it seems to be trying to do just that.

Why Newmont seems ready to stop shopping and start growing

Newmont seems to have changed gears. Firstly, they recently completed a massive USD 4.5 billion divestiture programme selling off assets to streamline their portfolio. Management stated that it is "satisfied" with the current portfolio and is now focussing on Project Catalyst - a cost reduction programme designed to shave off USD 100 million from general and administrative expenses (G&A). Their guidance for 2026 provides for a 21% reduction in G&A, from USD 475 million to USD 375 million. A significant part of this projected efficiency comes from workforce rationalisation following the mergers and divestment of assets - generally a ready "win" for the margins that just needs to be booked.

They also have declared a "trough year" where output is going to be lower in view of maintenance operations. The idea seems to be to capitalise on the cash flow surplus (a massive USD 7.3 billion in 2025) generated by increased asset prices to perform mine stripping at the Boddington and Peñasquito mines - using capital to move large quantities of waste rock out up front, with output projected to return to 6 million ounces of gold and 150,000 tonnes of copper by 2027. This means there's going to be an output hit for 2026, but 2027 is expected to see a strong bounce back. It has also used cash to extinguish some debt, leaving behind debt that is mainly low interest (some less than 3%).

Importantly, they have authorized a total of USD 6 billion buyback, indicating that the expansion era is likely done and the focus is now on consolidation and margins, and improving the free cash flow per share. Despite this, they expect to be in place to earn by-product credits from their copper assets in Cadia (Australia) and Red Chris (Canada), providing a margin floor mechanism that pure-gold players don't have. It's also worth noting that their major copper assets are in Tier-1 jurisdictions. New CEO Natascha Viljoen has explicitly made a 'green metal transition' towards a gold-copper hybrid part of her mandate at NEM - whether they are able to operationally iron out this strategy remains to be seen, but the fact is that neither Agnico nor AU have the polymetallic assets to pull this off, giving NEM a relatively wide margin of error to figure out execution. The only one trying to follow this playbook is Barrick, but Barrick is currently handicapped by its own problems, especially spiking cost and legal trouble with NEM that could affect its IPO plans as well.

Short term and long term

Over the last month, NEM has seen a slight pullback to USD 100 levels from USD 125 levels, driven by the steep correction in gold that has seen nearly all the gold miners pull back by 20% or more. Even during this pullback, we can see the trend of NEM and AU competing with roughly flat returns, while Agnico remains best in class for current returns (~14%) and the YTD for Barrick goes negative. In my opinion, AU remains a slightly overpriced pure metal play, which is fundamentally different from NEM as an investment.

2025 has been a good year for NEM, which saw it going from ~40 USD levels to ~125 USD levels before the current pullback. Given that most gold stocks, including Barrick followed the exact same trajectory, I do think that this was basically all gold price beta (see graph). However, analysts have also reacted positively to their new energised focus on operations and margins after a long decade of expansion. The current consensus target for Newmont is over USD 135. Seeking Alpha's own quant ratings are giving this a Strong Buy, and that is after downgrading growth and valuation over the last 3 months (it maintains a "very high" on profitability). JP Morgan is massively bullish on it as well, with a target of over USD 150.

With over 11 core assets and 3 more in development, and with the copper floor for gold and with different risks across jurisdictions, trying to do a back envelope P/NAV for this (as I often do for some junior miners with single assets) can be a bit futile in the limited space in this article. However, for anyone who wants to try it for themselves (which I strongly recommend for anyone considering this from a long-term perspective), you can use the reported reserves here.

For the purposes of this article, I was keener on demonstrating the 'why' of the bullish outlook on this stock than to give competing projections. In the short term, the recent pullback provides a fantastic kicker to the upside and a good entry point in my opinion. The management has provided pretty concrete guidance for a turnaround in 2027, and so you have two possible legs towards medium-term price growth - the recovery from USD 100 back to 125, and then analyst consensus suggests a further possible upside of at least another 10 dollars, up to USD 135, once the higher production numbers start getting reported. A second benefit is that the sheer size of the NEM acts as insurance. Next to Agnico, I would consider this to be the only true blue-chip of the mining sector. This means that I would be happy to pick this up for my 5-year portfolio without bothering too much about short-term asset price fluctuation or a few execution hiccups, which are both possible and inevitable to some degree. Agnico is an efficiency darling, but looking forward to the next 5 years, NEM's juicy pivot and explicit consolidation and operational focus give it a unique growth proposition that Agnico currently doesn't have (even if they are likely to find a strong investor hook eventually). If and when Agnico truly starts scaling up towards its next phase, NEM will have already played out for a nice 20%-30% upside.

Jurisdictional Risks

NEM's jurisdictional risks often get highlighted, and are real. However, the risk itself is already priced in, so their damper on growth arises mainly from delays caused by any actual triggers or events. Ghana's new sliding scale royalty system squeezes operating margins of its Ahafo North and South operations, which together contributes ~13% of the total revenue. Another ~13% comes from Peñasquito in Mexico, which is the source of massive silver, zinc and lead assets the company uses as revenue boosters and is also one of the main areas of capital deployment, with mine stripping operations in 2026 expected to lead to higher production in 2027. There seem to be no immediate issues there, but Mexico remains a security risk for all miners. Another 9% comes from the Lihir mine in PNG, and with other assets also slated for development this number is expected to rise, with PNG being a main source of growth for NEM. However, development plans have faced backlash from environmental group and locals. The US and PNG signed an investment de-risking agreement in 2025, which makes the probability of any dramatic downside relatively low, but there is always a risk of delays and complications.

Other than Ghana's higher royalty charge, however, most risks are potential rather than actual. A big mitigating factor to the jurisdictional risk is NEM's own experience as the adult in the room - it has some of the most extensive institutional memory of how to navigate frontier jurisdictions and some of the sharpest plays in the socio-politics of frontier mining - whether its integrating local Ghanaian chiefs during Ahafo North's development, integrating PNG-experienced Australian leadership and workforce or promoting tailings and water use standards that enable them to look good, navigate environmental concerns and effectively muscle out smaller players.

There are other standard operational risks, as well as the possibility of a correction in asset prices, but in the long term neither of these should be defining factors for NEM's growth. For a behemoth with sprawling operations, operational risks are both inevitable and manageable. They are hiccups and delays, not something that alters fundamentals. Meanwhile, common sense and consensus say that the asset price has corrected steeply in short term due to liquidity, not asset devaluation. And NEM is also strong on its polymetallic play in a way no other large miner is, giving it cushion against individual asset price shocks as opposed to a pure-play.

Conclusion

NEM feels a bit like a star performer late to the stage. After over a decade of spending and acquisitions, it has finally, and definitively, changed gears. It has been criticised for being slow and somewhat lethargic about its margins, about the price it has paid for its assets, about its multi-jurisdictional risks - but sometimes this criticism can overlook the fact that a century-old institution thinks in time frames and scales that leaner, meaner, younger competitors cannot. In a period when Barrick and AU were forced to scale back production, NEM kept up its volumes. It is working on a giant canvas across jurisdictions, painting it with more colours than just gold - and in doing that it is playing a game that probably only a company with its scale, size, and institutional history can play. Having finally indicated a shift from defense to offense could end up making this a very interesting decade for its investors. For the time being, it's offering a good price to enter at and a possible 20% return in the short term that investors can play for. If that goes well, they might want to consider if they have a bigger appetite for a big company making some big moves.

data.title: Beiersdorf: Taking Advantage Of The Sell-Off To Go Long
data.description:
Introduction

Beiersdorf (BDRFF) (BDRFY) is one of the world’s consumer goods companies with some iconic brands in its portfolio that is geared towards protecting and expanding its position in the skin care industry. The brand names NIVEA, Hansaplast, and Eucerin enjoy worldwide brand recognition and are the cornerstone brands in Beiersdorf’s portfolio. Meanwhile, the Tesa tape brand enjoys a good reputation in the business segment.

In order to maintain its position in its core markets, Beiersdorf has to continue to invest in R&D, which is one of the main expenses that are not directly related to SG&A or the actual COGS. These continued R&D efforts will allow Beiersdorf to launch new products this year.

Beiersdorf has its primary listing in Germany, where it is trading on Deutsche Boerse with BEI as its ticker symbol. The average daily volume in Germany is approximately half a million shares per day. Beiersdorf’s current market capitalization after the share price drop is approximately 16.1B EUR. I will use the Euro as base currency throughout this article.

Looking back at the 2025 results

Beiersdorf reported a pretty flat revenue in 2025, at 9.85B EUR. Unfortunately, the COGS increased by approximately 2% to 4.17B EUR, resulting in a 1.5% decrease in the gross profit.

As the income statement below shows, the marketing and selling expenses remain a very important cost. Fortunately, Beiersdorf was able to slightly reduce those expenses, and as you can see below, it was successful in keeping all of its operating expenses under control.

That helped to record a 2% EBIT increase, although that was mainly caused by a substantially lower ‘other operating expense,’ which decreased from exceeding half a billion Euro in the preceding year. As you can see below, a substantial portion of the other operating expenses are related to FX expenses (but this is compensated by a FX gain in the ‘other operating income’ result). The main reason for the lower ‘other expenses’ in FY 2025 is the sharp decrease in restructuring expenses and the amortization and impairment expenses related to the intangible assets from acquisitions.

Despite the lower interest rates, Beiersdorf was still able to post a net interest income as it has built up a large cash pile on its balance sheet. The net interest income of approximately 33M EUR (versus 27M EUR in FY 2024), of course, does help the bottom line result.

The net income generated in 2025 was 955M EUR of which 939M EUR was attributable to the common shareholders of Beiersdorf. This represented an EPS of 4.25 EUR, a 4.5% increase compared to the result in the preceding financial year.

When I invest in companies, I like to have a look at, and understand how the cash flow result supports the operations (and a company’s ability to continue to invest and/or pay dividends to its shareholders).

As the cash flow statement below shows, Beiersdorf reported an operating cash flow of just under 1.08B EUR before taking the changes in the working capital position into consideration. It’s also worth noting this includes a 477M EUR cash tax payment, although only 393M EUR was owed, based on the income statement. We should also deduct the 77M EUR in lease payments, 25M EUR in interest and other financing payments, as well as the 14M EUR in dividends paid to non-controlling interests. On the other hand, we should add 53M EUR from the cash interest income and the proceeds from finance leases and dividends.

Taking all these elements into account, the adjusted operating cash flow was approximately 1.09B EUR. We also know the capex was approximately 463M EUR, resulting in a net free cash flow of approximately 630M EUR.

Divided over the current share count of approximately 218M shares, this represents a free cash flow result of 2.89 EUR per share. Lower than the net income per share, mainly due to the difference between the depreciation and amortization expenses versus the total capex and lease payments.

The market was disappointed with the outlook for 2026

Beiersdorf’s outlook for 2026 was cautious. Too cautious for the market’s liking, as I think the somewhat careful outlook for the current financial year was somewhat unexpected.

I’m fine with what will shape up as a transition year, mainly because Beiersdorf has a rock solid balance sheet. At the end of 2025, the company had 1.2B EUR in cash, but also owned 1.26B EUR in current securities and 1.85B EUR in non-current securities. Meanwhile, the gross debt (excluding lease liabilities) is negligible. Although 1.85B EUR of the securities portfolio is classified as non-current, I’d argue it would be fine to include this in the calculation of the net cash position of the company, as their maximum term is four years.

This means the company has approximately 4.3B EUR in net cash on its balance sheet, and divided over 218M shares outstanding, this results in a net cash position of almost 20 EUR per share.

I’m also pleased to see Beiersdorf has announced a new 750M EUR share buyback program, to be completed over the next few years. As the share price is trading at one of the lowest levels in the past decade, I hope Beiersdorf is stepping up its buyback pace. Spending 400M EUR on buying back stock at the current share price level would allow the company to reduce the share count by another 2.5%.

Investment thesis

With a current market capitalization of 15.8B EUR and an enterprise value of approximately 11.5B EUR, Beiersdorf is currently trading at less than 7 times its EBITDA, making the stock quite attractive at these levels. I always found Beiersdorf too expensive, as it has been trading at EBITDA multiples of around 15 in the past few years, but I recently started writing put options (a combination of in the money and out of the money) on Beiersdorf with the clear intent to initiate a long position.

I may also buy shares on the open market in the near future, although that’s not likely to happen within the next 72 hours.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Synopsys: Elliot Stake Confirms Attraction, Reiterate Buy
data.description:
Introduction:

Synopsys (NASDAQ:SNPS) is a stock I think highly of, having previously assigned it a buy rating. Since my last coverage, the stock has simply put not delivered in terms of price performance. The stock found itself caught up in the recent market phenomenon known as SaaSpocalypse, or the disruption of all software vendors by AI for those unfamiliar with the term.

The shares look to be attractive based on historical valuation levels, and despite some lackluster execution of late by the company, investors got a boost this week with news that the renowned activist Elliott Investment Management had taken a multibillion-dollar stake in the company. This feels like a very good entry situation for me.

On one hand you have a strong historical compounder that operates in a market with few competitors, secular growth, and pricing power. That compounder finds itself underperforming the market, partly because it was wrongly caught up in a software sell-off in the market. Add to the mix a very serious activist fund with a track record of getting results, and I think that sets the shares up to perform well from here. A bit of longer-term perspective is important to remember: SNPS has delivered close to a 700% total return over the past decade vs. just 290% for the S&P 500.

I like SNPS, but I have to admit I expected more from the company in the past year. We are early in 2026, and I think it's shaping up to be far better than 2025. I am keeping my buy rating on SNPS.

SaaSpocalypse:

SaaSpocalypse, as it has been dubbed, relates to an aggressive sell-off in shares of software companies due to fears that AI would disrupt workflows and alter the economics of the software business model. This, I believe, is potentially true for some software vendors. I am thinking of discretionary, lightly integrated with core business functions, UI-driven software apps that are reliant on seat growth at the customer to continue to expand. These types of firms tend not to bring anything that might constitute a moat, making them ripe for disruption.

The other side of the coin is software vendors that have deep integration with their customers, often relying on customized workflows developed over many years. The very market structure in which SNPS participates tells us what type of software vendor it is. The Electronic Design Automation or EDA, market is one that is dominated by three large players: Synopsys, Cadence Design Systems (NASDAQ:CDNS), and Siemens (OTCPK:SIEGY). A market like this is highly specialized, with a few key incumbents serving as key partners for their customers. Contrast this with, say, the marketplace for HR or customer service software, for instance. These SaaS markets tend to be saturated with many single-point solutions.

Given the concentrated nature of the EDA market, vendors such as SNPS have long histories of working with customers and have a deep knowledge of a customer's needs, previous products, and future ambitions. The semiconductor industry is high stakes, and minor design flaws in new chips can lead to malfunctions and result in multi-million-dollar losses. As a result, the trust between chip design firms such as Intel (NASDAQ:INTC) and their EDA vendor is a critical bond, and I do not see it as representing a high likelihood of disruption.

Another key concern of the SaaS sell-off has been the ultimate effect on industry pricing models. Market commentators have suggested that SaaS providers that rely on seat-based pricing could face serious headwinds if customers end up using more agents and therefore less seats. The margin profile of such a move changes the equation as the software provider becomes a consumer of tokens to provide agents rather than simply a software provider to human users.

This is not a point of concern for SNPS; they do not run an atypical SaaS-type pricing model, and this is at the heart of why they are being incorrectly lumped in with other software vendors. The market has been selling software names as a theme or bucket, rather than looking at what individual names actually do, what market they operate in, what their moat is, and how they price the product.

Speaking at a recent Morgan Stanley (NYSE:MS) conference, CEO Sassine Ghazi answered this concern directly. Forgive the long quote, but I think it captures the point of pricing structure and AI very eloquently.

“It's not really a SaaS model. Today, the -- its license based on on-prem primarily. So the way our customers today assess how much money they spend with us is based on their number of engineers and the tasks they are running. So in many cases, you have an engineer running many, many licenses and other parts of the workflow; one engineer is running very few just because of the interaction and part of the workflow.

As far as the AI impact, AI is a great opportunity for us. It's actually driving the opportunity to monetize more. I mentioned earlier reinforcement learning, et cetera; those are techniques that we have a product for, and we're monetizing. And remember, what we deliver has a software layer that is driving deep physics, solvers, and engines that we do. Therefore, the AI opportunity to improve the user interface with generative AI, et cetera—we are leading with that innovation for our customers. With the next opportunity for agents, we are leading in building these agents with the solvers that we have. So we see it as absolutely a tailwind to simplify what our customers are trying to do, which is very complex already, both at the silicon level as well as the system level.” (Sassine Ghazi, CEO)

Elliott:

Elliott Investment Management is an activist investor known for taking stakes in companies and pushing for changes. The firm has run some extremely well-known and successful activist campaigns over the years. To give a few examples of their actions, in 2017 they took a stake in BHP (NYSE:BHP), the world's largest miner. At the time, BHP still ran an oil business, which Elliott pushed them to move away from. The campaign was successful, with BHP exiting the oil business to put greater focus on its core mining business, in particular copper.

Elliott took a stake in the industrial conglomerate Honeywell (NYSE:HON) in 2024; they pushed the company to break up into separate businesses to unlock value, a move similar to what was executed by General Electric, which turned into three separate businesses. Honeywell has begun the splitting process already, with its advanced materials business spun off, and the separation of the aerospace business is planned for later this year.

These examples, I hope, illustrate that Elliott means business when they get involved with companies. They are not taking stakes to sit idle; they have clearly identified either structural or operational improvements in advance of building a stake in the target company. Once they have a stake built, they get to work on implementing change. It will be interesting to see what changes are put forth for SNPS. Whatever Elliott has up their sleeve, I think there is a good chance it will be effective.

Quoted in the Wall Street Journal, Elliott partner Jesse Cohn said of SNPS

“Given the essential nature of its platform and these structural tailwinds, we believe there is a clear opportunity for Synopsys’ financial performance to more fully reflect the value it delivers,....We look forward to engaging with the company to help align operational execution, profitability, and monetization with its potential and importance to the semiconductor ecosystem." (Jesse Cohn, Elliott)

Valuation:

The valuation argument for SNPS is pretty straightforward right now, and I would assume it's a key driver in attracting a name like Elliott to become involved. SNPS trades at a steep discount to its recent valuation, and it is close to the bottom of its five-year P/E range, with the only lower level notched early last year as liberation day tariff fears were kicking off.

The valuation looks even more compelling when adjusted for growth with the PEG basis. The PEG number tells us that at no time in the past five years have we been able to purchase this level of expected growth for such a low price. The valuation is where it is for a reason: company execution has been so-so at best, there are concerns about the Ansys acquisition and justifying the price, and finally, the stock has been caught up in a wider sell-off.

This confluence of conditions creates a mispricing, as the market places too much emphasis on the near term, and too little consideration on the long-term track record. A reality that has obviously not been lost on an activist like Elliott. From their perspective, I imagine, they see all the negative narratives fully reflected in the stock price; the company remains set to deliver double-digit earnings growth for years to come; hence, from a risk-reward perspective, things look attractive.

Further to the above, we can see clear evidence of a divergence between the historical relationship between SNPS and CDNS. Given the long-term track record of both these operators and the similarities in the respective market share positions, I expect some tough love from engaged investors like Elliott should yield near-term results to help return this valuation divergence to a more normal level.

Risks:

On the Elliott side, there is a risk they cannot communicate effectively with the leadership of Synopsys, or indeed, maybe Elliott thinks they know what's best for the company, but given the complexity of EDA, they really do not. These activist campaigns usually lead to greater operational focus and better stock price performance, but there are no guarantees, of course. As an example, in 2017 Elliott tried to orchestrate the sale of Akzo Nobel (OTCQX:AKZOF) to rival PPG (NYSE:PPG). The proposed deal did not go down well with the management of Akzo, and ultimately nothing came of the deal. As they say, it takes two to tango. My hope is that Elliott brings a series of sensible and agreeable changes to the management of SNPS and the two parties can deliver on a path to improve the stock price performance, to the benefit of them personally and investors in general.

Conclusion:

My buy rating on SNPS remains in place. The stock has disappointed investors over the past year, but it has been a success longer-term. The stock has faced headwinds, but that has left it at arguably the most attractive valuation since the turn of the 2020s. The highly attractive entry point coupled with the new position of Elliott suggests to me the stock is well placed to perform from here. It hasn't been a clean story; markets have had their concerns, but the discounted valuation suggests a majority of pessimism is already reflected in the price. In my opinion, SNPS represents a good buy at these levels.

data.title: MillerKnoll: Another Major Post-Earnings Pullback, But I'm Uneasy This Time (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Back in September 2025, I wrote a bullish article about MillerKnoll (MLKN) after the stock had a post-earnings plunge. At that time the stock was trading for about $17, and I suggested accumulating it over time. The stock ended up going even lower into the end of 2025, but then sharply rallied and traded up to around $23 in February 2026. I sold my shares when the stock rallied, but once again, it has plunged after reporting earnings. I like buying stocks of solid companies after major declines because it can be rewarding to buy stocks when they reach oversold levels and potentially trade at bargain valuations. In recent quarters, there seems to be a pattern with MillerKnoll whereby the stock plunges after earnings are released, and this is concerning. However, there also seems to be a pattern whereby the stock rebounds. I see this company as having very valuable brands and a reputation for high quality, and that, along with a generous dividend, is what I find attractive. However, I am increasingly concerned with repeated post-earnings stock plunges and also with macroeconomic concerns. With all of this in mind, let's take an updated look at this stock:

The Chart

As shown in the chart below, this stock is volatile, and it almost doubled in value from the lows of about $13 in November last year as it surged to nearly $23 in February. But, once again, it has plunged and now trades for about $15 per share. This stock is now at oversold levels, and it trades well below the 50-day moving average, which is around $20, and the 200-day moving average, which is just over $18. This stock seems to have found some support in the $14 to $15 range, but this is very close to lows it hit in the $13 range late last year, so I would not be surprised if it retests those lows, especially as the stock market continues to be under pressure from the war in Iran.

Q3 Earnings Results

On March 26, MillerKnoll announced Q3 earnings results, which showed that non-GAAP came in at $0.43 per share, missing estimates by $0.02. For the quarter, the company generated revenues of $926.6 million, which also missed estimates by about $15 million. On the positive side, gross margins increased by 20 basis points. Considering the slowdown in the U.S. economy and that bad weather impacted many companies, I think these results were pretty solid. It's important to note that the company is still dealing with the impact of tariffs, but the company says it has fully mitigated these expenses and expects this to continue for the rest of 2026.

In terms of guidance for Q4, the company expects net sales to range between $955 million and $995 million, which represents a 1.4% increase when compared to last year. The company expects gross margins to come in at between 37.5% and 38.5%, due to higher expected shipping and other expenses because of the surging price of oil. This is one of the first hints I have seen that shows companies are expecting to be impacted by the war in Iran, so we could see a lot more of this when earnings season gets into full swing in April. The company estimates that the direct impact of the war in Iran will be around $8 to $9 million for Q4, and that equates to around $0.09 to $0.10, on a per share basis.

Other concerns for the rest of this year could include weakness in government contract sales and what could be weakening consumer and business sentiment because of AI-related job layoffs and geopolitical risks. Since the Iran war started, interest rates have climbed higher, and I am concerned that this might negatively impact business and consumer spending, as well as drag down the economy overall. The financial markets have even started to suggest that the Fed's next move could be to increase interest rates in response to the rising price of oil and presumably inflation. I don't see this as being the most likely next step for the Fed, but if it does happen, the entire stock market could come under more selling pressure.

Earnings Estimates and The Balance Sheet

As shown below in the earnings estimates provided by Seeking Alpha, MillerKnoll is expected to earn about $1.82 per share in 2026. Earnings are expected to grow in 2027 and 2028, although revenue growth is expected to remain fairly minimal. Based on an implied price to earnings ratio of just over 8 times estimates for 2026, this stock looks like a bargain to me. But, I am concerned that valuation levels could remain low for a few reasons, which include the potential for this company to be facing structural challenges if AI job losses continue to mount, and also because of recession concerns.

As for the balance sheet, MillerKnoll has about $1.78 billion in debt and nearly $175 million in cash. I was concerned with this debt load previously, and I am more concerned about it now since I believe the risks of a recession are growing. I also am getting more concerned as the company gets closer to debt maturities. As shown below, the company does have some potentially significant debt maturities coming up in FY2030 and FY2033:

My Top Concerns With MillerKnoll

While tariffs and the war in Iran have been more immediate concerns, I am worried about a potential structural change in the office furniture market due to increasing layoffs that are related to AI. This company derives a significant amount of business from white-collar jobs, and that is the segment of the job market that could be hit hard in the coming years. We have seen software stocks plunge in value recently due to AI disruption concerns. Part of this is happening because investors are worried that fewer humans will be doing any tasks that AI can do, and this means fewer "seats" at the office. That sure seems like a direct threat to a company that literally sells seats and other office furniture. A recent report suggests that about 55,000 job losses in 2025 were related to AI, and that AI job losses could be 9 times more in 2026. This shows massive growth in AI-related job losses, and we are still so early in the AI revolution. I fear this could get much worse, and then in the next couple of years, we could even see more job losses when humanoid robots potentially go mainstream.

While I believe the stock market is oversold now and probably due for a short-term rebound, I am increasingly convinced that the economy could slip into a recession in the coming months. I recently wrote an article about a few indicators that have accurately predicted recessions in the past. Recession indicators such as the Sahm Rule and the yield curve have recently been triggered, and this could mean a recession is coming. A recession could exacerbate the already weak job market, and this stock could be very negatively impacted. The low PE ratio that we see with this stock today could be a sign that the market expects earnings to decline significantly in the coming years due to AI job losses and a potential recession.

Some Positives To Consider

I believe this company has an amazing portfolio of brand names, many of which are recognized globally. Some of its brands and designs are also historically iconic. It's possible that some of these brands are worth much more than this company is getting credit for. I believe this company could be an attractive takeover target based on its brand portfolio. Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A), (BRK.B) has shown interest in the furniture industry, especially when it comes to strong brands. It owns the Nebraska Furniture Mart and has a stake in Restoration Hardware (RH). It wouldn't surprise me to see a company like Berkshire Hathaway take a stake in a company like MillerKnoll. In addition to these points, MillerKnoll has a number of other positives going for it, which the company highlights below:

Where I Could Be Wrong

The market is going through a correction now, but with many major indices down by about 10% or more in recent weeks, this could be a buying opportunity for many stocks. I do expect the war in Iran to de-escalate sooner rather than later, and this could lead to a major market rebound. Many consumers are starting to receive larger income tax refunds this year, and that could lead to a boost in consumer spending and higher GDP growth in the coming quarters. The valuation of this stock looks extremely cheap when considering the PE ratio, and the company has a history of working through a variety of challenges. I believe the brands that this company has are very valuable, and I even feel that this company could be a takeover target or that it could sell one or more of its brands for a significant sum. Based on this, I believe this company could be worth more than the current market cap suggests.

In Summary

I was more eager to buy the post-earnings plunges with this stock in the past, but it seems to be happening too often, and my concerns about a recession and the debt load that this company has are growing. I am still intrigued by what I view as a very strong brand portfolio that this company has and the very low PE ratio at which this stock is currently trading. The dividend yield of roughly 5% is also enticing. But, I think it just makes sense to hold this stock for now. Perhaps, if it drifts even lower and retests the lows of about $13, which it hit last year, I would consider buying a small position.

data.title: Analyst Connect March 2026: Guidelines For Politics And The Markets
data.description:
Markets and Geopolitics: Guidelines for Volatile Times

Markets and politics often intersect, and they crossed paths big time in March with the Iran conflict. We want to remind analysts of our guidelines and how to approach geopolitical topics in a challenging environment:

  • Make sure to focus on how a political development may impact the macro picture, the stock market, or a specific investment idea.

  • Make sure any investment or macro takeaway is actionable.

  • Make sure to provide support and sourcing from credible sources (and consider using Seeking Alpha’s news coverage as a source; see details on sourcing below).

Most importantly, make sure any political commentary does not distract or overwhelm your macro or investment-specific assessment.

What we avoid:

  • Articles should not take a position on the merits of a policy or politician. And we won’t present articles that contain an attack on a politician, a policy, or other analysts.

  • We’ll pass on articles that focus on a political topic without a direct connection to an investment or a clearly defined macro theme.

  • We won’t present content that contains unsupported speculation or allegations regarding intent.

  • And we don't present politically based acronyms within analysis articles. That includes "TACO," "BigMAC" and "TUNA."

Once an article is posted, make sure to remain respectful of opposing viewpoints. We rely on analysts to ensure that article comments remain polite and useful for all readers.

Our guidelines on politics and the markets can be found here.

Secondary Tickers: Details Matter

Adding in secondary tickers to an article puts more eyeballs on the content. Articles with secondary tickers will appear in the "Related Analysis" section of that ticker’s profile page. And users who have that ticker in a portfolio will see the article appear on their "My Portfolio" page.

Our guidelines for secondary tickers:

  • Articles should present a decent amount of detail on any company with a secondary ticker designation. Just a single, passing mention or inclusion in a chart or table does not warrant secondary ticker placement.

  • Key items such as current stock price, a quick look at key valuation metrics, or mentions of a material development or catalyst can help ensure a ticker gets secondary ticker placement. The details can be brief, but they still need to be in the article.

  • Any stock mentioned in an article and given a secondary ticker placement should be directly relevant to the analysis. There needs to be a connection between the main stock in focus and the secondary ticker. This includes tickers in the same sector, but also can include stocks (or other investments such as ETFs) that can be compared based on items such as valuation, dividends, or any other related factor.

  • We encourage analysts to present a peer/competitor comparison in articles. A detailed comparison will likely get any peer listed as a primary ticker. Otherwise, the ticker could instead be listed as secondary. Please see our Primary Tickers Policy for more information on multiple primary tickers.

Editors have discretion when it comes to the use of secondary tickers. Feel free to present a note to editors explaining why you want to list certain tickers as secondaries.

Please note our guidelines on preferred shares and other investments such as baby bonds. We invite coverage of preferred shares and related investments, along with the common stock of a company. Use of a preferred stock as the main focus must provide in-depth analysis for primary ticker designation. If the content isn’t in depth, it’s likely that the preferred shares will be designated as a secondary ticker. We don’t allow for secondary ticker designation for preferred shares that don’t get mentioned in an article. And, again, editors have discretion when it comes to secondary ticker use.

The best advice we can provide about secondary tickers? Don’t present a lengthy list - avoid "spamming" with secondary ticker designations. Present a reasonable selection that’s relevant to the analysis.

These secondary ticker guidelines apply only to articles in which a single stock is the focus (a primary ticker), not for "lists" articles. We’ll present guidelines on lists articles at a later date.

Sourcing: Support Your Analysis With the Proper Links

We encourage strong sourcing in article content. Whether those sources are offsite or on Seeking Alpha, links to pertinent and supportive sources create a stronger article.

Our guidelines for outside links:

  • We allow up to three links to Web pages directly associated with an analyst.

  • Please note, however, links to an analyst’s external site cannot contain pop-up marketing solicitations and "subscribe" buttons.

  • We do not allow links to Substack pages given that these pages contain pop-ups, subscribe buttons, or other solicitations.

  • We prefer that outside links do not go to paywalled content. For paywalled content, we recommend that analysts clearly identify the source along with pertinent information such as the name of the source and date of the sourced material.

  • Do not use AI tools to locate sources.

There’s no limit to the number of links to articles written by analysts on Seeking Alpha. But we suggest limiting these links only to relevant and timely/recent submissions. Editors can exercise discretion with regard to these article links.

We strongly recommend analysts use resources on Seeking Alpha as much as possible. Our individual ticker pages contain tabs for our news coverage, transcripts, press releases, and SEC filings. Analysts are encouraged to link to these sources when available, rather than linking to offsite sources.

Titles and Questions: Why We Avoid This Format

As a reminder, we don’t allow for a question format in titles. This type of format appears to readers as potential clickbait. And the question format often leaves out key details about the article’s premise.

This includes rhetorical question formats.

We’ll make limited exceptions based on the article content, the quality of the article, and if the question format provides some context about the analysis. Please provide a note to editors explaining the need for a question format in a title.

We updated our title guidelines in the February Analyst Connect newsletter, which can be found here.

Working Overseas: One Analyst’s Experience on Seeking Alpha

Luca Socci, a Seeking Alpha analyst since 2022, is based in Italy. Being overseas gives this analyst a unique perspective on the markets and investing. This month, Socci shares his work experience, what’s similar in his approach compared to other analysts, and what may be different.

His advice for other overseas analysts and those based in North America: Use your unique talents to put a bigger spotlight on your work. Focus on quality first.

His perspective can be found here.

Looking Ahead to April

As we move into April, the earnings reporting activity is rather slow for the first few days, but then ramps up as the month goes on. We will enter the spring earnings season where most companies will be reporting results for the calendar first quarter of 2026. The first full week of the month is when things kick off and the quantity of reports increases gradually throughout the month. As for the earnings reports, please keep in mind that as we get further out in the month, some of the earnings dates haven’t been confirmed by the companies yet and these are estimated dates.

Seeking Alpha Earnings Calendar

Week 1 (April 1-3)

During the first few days of the month, there isn’t a single company set to report with a market cap over $10B. April 3 is a market holiday in observance of Good Friday and there aren’t any earnings reports that day. As for Wednesday and Thursday, the biggest names reporting are Conagra (CAG) and Lamb Weston (LW) on Wednesday and Acuity (AYI) on Thursday.

Week 2 (April 6-10)

The week sees an increase in the number of large-cap names reporting and it kicks off on Wednesday when we expect to hear from Delta Airlines (DAL), Constellation Brands (STZ), RPM International (RPM), and Applied Digital (APLD). There aren’t any large caps expected to report on Thursday or Friday.

Week 3 (April 13-17)

Week 3 is when we see what many would consider the kickoff to the second quarter earnings season as the biggest banks in the U.S. unofficially start the action. Goldman Sachs (GS) will kick things off on Monday morning with Fastenal (FAST) also reporting. Moving to Tuesday, April 14, things really start to heat up with a number of $100B companies set to report. Those names include JPMorgan Chase (JPM), Wells Fargo (WFC), and Citigroup (C) from the banking sector and Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) from the healthcare sector.

Looking out to Wednesday, we get reports from more major financial institutions like Bank of America (BAC), Morgan Stanley (MS), and PNC Financial (PNC) along with chip giant ASML (ASML). Shifting our focus to Thursday, we see more banking names – Bank of New York Mellon (BK, U.S. Bancorp (USB), but we also get reports from Netflix (NFLX) and Alcoa (AA). Moving out to Friday we see more financial sector companies like Truist (TFC), Fifth Third (FITB) and Ally (ALLY), but we also have oil service giant SLB (SLB).

Week 4 (April 20-24)

This is when we start getting less certainty about the actual dates as many companies haven’t announced or posted when the earnings will be released on their investor relations pages. Regardless, we see that there are some companies with rather large market caps and that are highly followed reporting during the week. On Monday, Steel Dynamics (STLD) is expected to report and then on Tuesday we expect to hear from GE Aerospace (GE), RTX (RTX), Intuitive Surgical (ISRG), and Halliburton (HAL).

Moving out to Wednesday, Philip Morris International (PM), GE Vernova (GEV), IBM (IBM), AT&T (T), and CME Group are all expected to report. On Thursday, we expect to get results from heavyweights like American Express (AXP), SAP (SAP), Honeywell (HON), Newmont (NEM), L3Harris (LHX), and Baker Hughes. On Friday, look for reports from AbbVie (ABBV), HCA Healthcare (HCA), and Charter Communications (CHTR).

Week 5 (April 27-30)

The final week of the month is when we expect to start hearing from the Magnificent 7 cohort, but not right out of the gate. On Monday, we see names like Verizon (VZ), Eaton (ETN) and Southern Copper (SCCO) on the docket. Tuesday marks the uptick in the mega cap reports with Alphabet (GOOG), Visa (V), AMD (AMD) Coca-Cola (KO) and Novartis (NVS) all expected that day.

Wednesday and Thursday see a big jump in total reports (196 on Wednesday, 243 on Thursday). We expect to hear from Amazon (AMZN) and Meta (META) on Wednesday, along with AstraZeneca (AZN), KLA (KLAC), Qualcomm (QCOM), and Sandisk (SNDK). Looking out to Thursday, watch for reports from behemoths like Apple (AAPL), Eli Lilly (LLY), Mastercard (MA), Caterpillar (CAT), Merck (MRK) and Amgen (AMGN).
